Just to add context (no pun intended), OpenAI also charge differently based on context usage with GPT 5.5 being $5/30 below 200K, and $10/45 above.

These per-token pricing schemes aren't directly comparable though since these models all use different numbers of tokens, even for input (Anthropic's recent tokenizer change generates 30% more tokens for exact same input), as well as for reasoning, and context/token usage also varies wildly by harness with Claude Code using 3x the context/tokens of Pi.

children.author: staticshock
children.textRawHtml:
Think of the entirety of the context (the full thread of conversation, all tool call output, etc.) as one message that's been submitted to the LLM all at once just so it can generate the next token (which is just the next word or even syllable.) Once that token is generated it is appended to the context, and the entire context is once again used to generate the next token. Keep doing that until the entire response is generated. The larger the context, the more stuff the model has to pay attention to as it generates each next token.

To use an analogy: imagine your friend is the author of an unfinished book. They die with 19 chapters written, and on their death bed ask you to write the 20th chapter. Assuming you're up to the task, you can only do this well if you take the time to absorb the entirety of what's been written so far.

This is how LLMs work. Context caching is an optimization on top of this, but it has its limits.
children.textZhHtml:
将整个上下文（对话的完整线程、所有工具调用输出等）视为一次性提交给 LLM 的一条消息，以便它可以生成下一个标记（这只是下一个单词甚至音节。）生成该标记后，它会附加到上下文，并且整个上下文再次用于生成下一个标记。继续这样做，直到生成整个响应。上下文越大，模型在生成每个下一个标记时需要注意的内容就越多。

打个比方：想象你的朋友是一本未完成的书的作者。他们死时写下了 19 章，临终时要求你写第 20 章。假设您能够胜任这项任务，那么只有花时间吸收到目前为止所写的全部内容，您才能做好这项工作。

这就是法学硕士的工作方式。上下文缓存是对此的优化，但它有其局限性。

children.author: HarHarVeryFunny

I guess it does increase their cost, or rather your share of their hardware depreciation.

AI serving cost is apparently mostly hardware depreciation rather than operating cost (electricity etc), and if your large context request is occupying VRAM for some fraction of a second then you are paying for the depreciation that occurs in that time!

e. H100 costs $20-40K to buy, with a lifetime of maybe 3 years, and will only consume maybe $2K in electricity if run 24x7 for those 3 years.
children.textZhHtml:

AI 服务成本显然主要是硬件折旧而不是运营成本（电力等），如果你的大型上下文请求占用 VRAM 一小部分时间，那么你就要为当时发生的折旧付费！

children.author: londons_explore
children.textRawHtml:
There are various difficulties with renting GPUs, especially if your setup is very custom.

The competitor would have to port their training systems to your specific network architecture, system design, rdma Vs ethernet vs infiniband Vs nvlink etc.

Getting it running might not be too hard, but getting it running efficiently and making good use of all those flops will require considerable human effort and wall time.

Add that to the fact most frontier labs seem to have a single huge training run - and to my knowledge nobody has figured out how to distribute that training run between data centers effectively.
children.textZhHtml:
租用 GPU 会遇到各种困难，尤其是在您的设置非常自定义的情况下。

竞争对手必须将其训练系统移植到您的特定网络架构、系统设计、rdma、以太网、infiniband、nvlink 等。

让它运行可能不会太难，但让它高效运行并充分利用所有这些失败将需要大量的人力和时间。

添加事实上，大多数前沿实验室似乎都有一次巨大的训练运行 - 据我所知，没有人知道如何在数据中心之间有效地分配该训练运行。

stories.title: Microsoft fire idTech team at Id software
stories.titleZh: 微软解雇了 Id software 的 idTech 团队

comments.author: ndiddy
comments.textRawHtml: I think we'll see stuff like this continue to happen over time. As a game company, having your own engine means that you have to be able to cultivate internal expertise in your tooling. Your employees will know this and could do bad things like ask for more money because they know that replacing them would significantly hurt productivity. Meanwhile, laying off your whole engine team and switching to UE5 means that you can get access to tons of low-wage contractors who know UE5. You can hire a bunch of them when you start a game project and then lay them all off when it's finished, and rinse and repeat as necessary. It lets you treat your employees as a replaceable commodity that can be scaled up and down as it makes monetary sense rather than a cohesive team of skilled artisans.
comments.textZhHtml: 我认为随着时间的推移，我们会看到类似的事情继续发生。作为一家游戏公司，拥有自己的引擎意味着您必须能够培养工具方面的内部专业知识。您的员工会知道这一点，并且可能会做坏事，例如索要更多钱，因为他们知道更换他们会严重损害生产力。同时，解雇整个引擎团队并切换到 UE5 意味着您可以接触到大量了解 UE5 的低薪承包商。当你开始一个游戏项目时，你可以雇佣一群人，然后在项目完成后将他们全部解雇，并根据需要进行冲洗和重复。它可以让您将您的员工视为一种可替换的商品，可以根据金钱的需要而扩大或缩小规模，而不是一个有凝聚力的熟练工匠团队。

children.author: munificent
children.textRawHtml:
All of this is true and has been true for decades in the game industry.

The other side of this seesaw is: Games are fundamentally in the novelty business. Players like some amount of familiarity, but they want new experiences. Every game engine has a sort of "grain" to it where it tends to produce games with a certain look and feel. The flat-ish shading and floaty physics of Unity is a particularly visible example of this. So using a widely used game engine can put you at a disadvantage if you're trying to make a game that doesn't go with that grain and offers players something different.

As more studios consolidate on the same engine, more players will get tired of that sameness and reward other studios more. As more studios do their own thing, players will become saturated with novelty and the benefits of not using an engine will go down. There is no stable equilibrium.
children.textZhHtml:
所有这些都是事实，并且在游戏行业几十年来一直如此。

这个跷跷板的另一面是：游戏从根本上来说是一种新奇行业。玩家喜欢一定程度的熟悉感，但他们想要新的体验。每个游戏引擎都有一种“颗粒”，它往往会产生具有特定外观和感觉的游戏。 Unity 的扁平化着色和浮动物理就是一个特别明显的例子。因此，如果您试图制作一款不符合这种要求并为玩家提供不同东西的游戏，那么使用广泛使用的游戏引擎可能会让您处于不利地位。

随着越来越多的工作室整合到同一个引擎上，越来越多的玩家会厌倦这种千篇一律的做法，并向其他工作室提供更多奖励。随着越来越多的工作室做自己的事情，玩家将被新奇事物所饱和，而不使用引擎的好处将会下降。不存在稳定平衡。

children.author: Cpoll
children.textRawHtml:
> Every game engine has a sort of "grain" to it where it tends to produce games with a certain look and feel.

I think this is a bit of a myth. Unreal gets this criticism a lot, but it's usually because many studios choose to stick close to the rendering defaults, which does lead to a certain look.

To that point, it's probably a lot cheaper to configure Unreal or Unity into a unique "grain" than it is to develop your own engine. It's also possible to use custom physics instead of those built into the engine.
children.textZhHtml:
> 每个游戏引擎都有一种“颗粒”，它往往会产生具有特定外观和感觉的游戏。

我认为这有点神话。虚幻经常受到这种批评，但这通常是因为许多工作室选择坚持渲染默认值，这确实导致了某种外观。

就这一点而言，将虚幻或 Unity 配置为独特的“颗粒”可能比开发自己的引擎便宜得多。还可以使用自定义物理场而不是引擎中内置的物理场。

children.author: willis936
children.textRawHtml: I can tell you that the fact that id tech 6 engine games look as good as they do and pump out 200+ fps is pretty novel in a way that the massive epic games and their flagship UE5 (and no painstakingly optimized case studies one may conjure) come close to matching even a decade on.  The reward for lapping the entire industry?  Early retirement.
children.textZhHtml: 我可以告诉你，id tech 6 引擎游戏看起来和它们一样好，并且输出 200+ fps，这在某种程度上是相当新颖的，大型史诗游戏和他们的旗舰 UE5（人们可能不会想到任何精心优化的案例研究）甚至在十年后都接近匹配。  包揽整个行业的回报是什么？  提前退休。

children.author: ahartmetz
children.textRawHtml: I have also been very impressed by the performance and fluidity of Doom and Doom Eternal. Even when playing from a HDD (on a computer made from leftover parts), when high res textures and geometry didn't load fast enough, the engine just showed a low res version before smoothly blending to higher res without ever stuttering. It's a minor miracle how well these games run, even on GPUs 2-3 tiers lower than most others require.
children.textZhHtml: 《毁灭战士》和《毁灭战士：永恒》的性能和流畅性也给我留下了深刻的印象。即使从 HDD（在由剩余部件制成的计算机上）播放时，当高分辨率纹理和几何图形加载速度不够快时，引擎也只会显示低分辨率版本，然后平滑地混合到更高的分辨率，而不会出现卡顿。即使在比大多数其他游戏所需的 GPU 低 2-3 层的情况下，这些游戏的运行效果也能如此出色，这简直就是一个小奇迹。

children.author: midnightclubbed
children.textRawHtml:
id tech is probably the last engine that used a signature game to showcase the engine to licensees. The business model of idTech, Unreal Engine, and Crytech used to be 'make amazing looking game that sells the engine to licensees'. The problem with that model was you got a cut of the game code for whatever the 'amazing looking' game was and told to figure it out. If you wanted to make a Gears of War clone then Unreal Engine 3 was perfect... for every other game genre you had to rewrite and implement huge chunks of code.

Unreal Engine managed to evolve and expand the engine to the point where it is somewhat game agnostic, crytech became lumberyard under Amazon and died, idTech became the engine solely for iD games (ie the same business model as every other game or publisher custom engine).

iD tech's rendering pipeline is focussed on the one AA game iD are publishing that year and making that one game run efficiently and beautifully on mid-high end PC and current consoles. UE5 is focussed on providing tools for anyone to (relatively) easily make good looking games and applications that can be published to a wide range of devices.
children.textZhHtml:
id tech 可能是最后一个使用签名游戏向授权商展示引擎的引擎。 idTech、Unreal Engine 和 Crytech 的商业模式曾经是“制作外观精美的游戏，然后将引擎出售给授权商”。 该模型的问题在于，无论“外观令人惊叹”的游戏是什么，您都会获得游戏代码的一部分，并被告知要弄清楚它。 如果你想制作《战争机器》的克隆版，那么虚幻引擎 3 是完美的...对于你必须重写和实现大量代码的其他游戏类型。

虚幻引擎成功地发展和扩展了引擎，使其在某种程度上与游戏无关，crytech 成为亚马逊旗下的木材场并消亡，idTech 成为专门用于 iD 游戏的引擎（即与所有其他游戏或发行商自定义引擎相同的商业模式）。

iD tech 的渲染管道是专注于 iD 当年发布的一款 AA 游戏，并让这款游戏在中高端 PC 和当前游戏机上高效、美观地运行。 UE5 专注于为任何人提供工具（相对）轻松地制作可发布到各种设备的精美游戏和应用程序。

children.author: danbolt
children.textRawHtml:
I think the big reason they get such good framerates is their conservatism with shader counts. A staff member mentions that in an interview with DigitalFoundry for Doom: The Dark Ages. I wonder if their in-house technology made such restraint easier.

Compared to other engines, making a new material asset is easy for someone less technical to do willy-nilly.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为他们获得如此好的帧率的一个重要原因是他们对着色器计数的保守态度。一位工作人员在接受 DigitalFoundry 的《毁灭战士：黑暗时代》采访时提到了这一点。我想知道他们的内部技术是否使这种限制变得更容易。

与其他引擎相比，制造新的物质资产对于技术水平较低的人来说很容易随心所欲地做。

children.author: midnightclubbed
children.textRawHtml:
For sure, if you restrict what can (and can't) be done in shaders you can optimize buffer layouts and rendering passes, not allow things that explode shader permutations/switching or explode shader instruction count and kill occupancy.

The flip side is the artists loose control and cannot author custom materials.
children.textZhHtml:
当然，如果您限制着色器中可以（和不可以）执行的操作，您可以优化缓冲区布局和渲染通道，不允许发生破坏着色器排列/切换或破坏着色器指令计数并杀死占用的情况。

另一方面是艺术家失去控制，无法创作自定义材质。

children.author: flohofwoe
children.textRawHtml:
> The flip side is the artists loose control and cannot author custom materials.

...which is a good thing tbh. No game needs hundreds or thousands of custom materials.
children.textZhHtml:
> 另一方面是艺术家控制松散，无法创作自定义材料。

...说实话，这是一件好事。没有游戏需要数百或数千种自定义材料。

children.author: cyber_kinetist
children.textRawHtml: I think it can actually be a bit better in terms of artistic consistency to have a fixed set of materials you can work with in a given game. Very important if you want any sort of stylized rendering!
children.textZhHtml: 我认为在艺术一致性方面，拥有一组可以在给定游戏中使用的固定材料实际上会更好一些。如果您想要任何类型的风格化渲染，这一点非常重要！

children.author: WillPostForFood
children.textRawHtml:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Id_Tech_6#Games_using_id_Tech_...

It may have been great, and lapped the entire industry, but it wasn't getting used very much, even internally at Microsoft. So how much value was really bringing?
children.textZhHtml:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Id_Tech_6#Games_using_id_Tech_...

它可能很棒，并且席卷了整个行业，但它并没有被广泛使用，甚至在微软内部也是如此。那么真正带来了多少价值？

children.author: canelonesdeverd
children.textRawHtml: "How much value can this bring?" Is the question that the incompetents at microslop needed to ask themselves before absorbing half the industry, not the smug justification for killing great tech after failing to capitalize on it. Specially when it brought great value to the industry for decades.
children.textZhHtml: "这能带来多少价值？"这是 microslop 的无能者在吸收一半行业之前需要问自己的问题，而不是在未能利用伟大技术后扼杀伟大技术的自鸣得意的理由。特别是当它数十年来为行业带来了巨大价值时。

children.author: NooneAtAll3
children.textRawHtml:
> usually because many studios choose to stick close to the rendering defaults, which does lead to a certain look

that's exactly the argument?

you can do anything if you put enough effort in - but effort is finite, so in aggregate the distribution will cluster around the defaults
children.textZhHtml:
> 通常是因为许多工作室选择接近渲染默认值，这确实会导致某种外观

这正是争论的焦点？

如果你付出足够的努力，你可以做任何事情 - 但努力是有限的，所以总的来说，分布将聚集在默认值周围

children.author: jdnfmfmfm

children.textRawHtml: I can pick out a creation engine game from a mile away. Engine "Grain" is a real phenomenon. Same for UE5. There's just something about the lighting and the FPS 'feel' that is a dead giveaway.
children.textZhHtml: 我可以从一英里外挑选出一款创作引擎游戏。发动机“颗粒”是一个真实的现象。 UE5 也一样。灯光和 FPS 的“感觉”完全暴露了一切。

children.author: RajT88
children.textRawHtml:

(Not being snarky - legit question)
children.textZhHtml:

stories.title: Why skilled workers come to Germany and then leave again
stories.titleZh: 为什么技术工人来到德国后又会再次离开

comments.author: ikut3hva
comments.textRawHtml:
Hi everyone

I don’t usually comment on topics like this because there are so many biases and different perspectives involved. In the end, I believe only the person who has actually gone through the experience can truly understand it; otherwise, it often becomes just another judgment.

We are an ASEAN family earning more than €200k gross annually (sorry for mentioning the TC, but there is a reason for it—please keep reading before judging). We have lived here for more than six years, and you know what? I still haven’t obtained either permanent residence or German citizenship simply because I don’t have a B1 certificate. So first things first: regardless of how much you contribute to the country, German is a must today if you want to obtain residency and stabilize your life here.

I was honestly devastated when the officer told me that I was not eligible for permanent residence. That was also the moment when I started to feel that maybe I don’t actually need permanent residence in this country after all.

Story 2: In an international working environment, German may not matter much at the IC level. But I’ve seen countless situations where Germans exchange a glance with each other, and suddenly the final decision is not what was agreed upon in the meeting. Over time, I’ve learned that there are many unwritten rules behind the scenes, and when you speak their language, you start to understand them.

One bright thing is that maybe we’re still lucky. We bought our first home without fully understanding the laws, the government system, or the tax rules. We simply worked hard and played the game in a way that we believed would be sustainable in the long run. Whatever happens, we know there are still many other places we could go.

Our children speak German natively, but they are also willing to go the extra mile to speak our mother tongue at home.

If you ask me for one piece of advice for immigrants and emigrants in Germany, I’d say: life is short—play naked!
comments.textZhHtml:
大家好

我通常不会评论这样的话题，因为其中涉及很多偏见和不同的观点。最后，我相信只有真正经历过的人才能真正理解；否则，它往往会成为另一个判断。

我们是一个年收入超过20万欧元的东盟家庭（很抱歉提到TC，但这是有原因的——请在判断之前继续阅读）。我们在这里住了六年多了，你知道吗？我仍然没有获得永久居留权或德国公民身份，只是因为我没有 B1 证书。因此，首先要做的事情是：无论您为国家做出了多少贡献，如果您想获得居留权并在这里稳定生活，德语都是必须的。

当官员告诉我我没有资格获得永久居留权时，我真的感到很沮丧。也是在那一刻，我开始觉得也许我实际上并不需要在这个国家永久居留。

故事 2：在国际工作环境中，德语在 IC 层面上可能并不重要。但我见过无数次这样的情况：德国人互相交换了一个眼神，突然间最终的决定并不是会议上达成的一致。随着时间的推移，我了解到幕后有许多不成文的规则，当你说他们的语言时，你开始理解它们。

一件好事是，也许我们仍然很幸运。我们在没有完全了解法律、政府制度或税收规则的情况下购买了第一套房子。我们只是努力工作，并以我们认为从长远来看可持续的方式玩游戏。无论发生什么，我们知道我们还有很多其他地方可以去。

我们的孩子的母语是德语，但他们也愿意在家里多花点力气说我们的母语。

如果你问我对德国的移民和移民有什么建议，我会说：生命短暂——赤身裸体！

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml:
I appreciate your perspective, but I was curious what B1 proficiency actually entails and this is what I found [1]:

- understand the main points of clear standard speech on familiar topics such as work, school, or leisure - manage most situations that occur while traveling in German-speaking areas - produce simple, connected text on familiar subjects - describe experiences, events, dreams, hopes, and ambitions, and briefly explain your opinions or plans

That seems like a reasonable standard of native language proficiency to ask of people who want to make the county with said language their permanent home.

[1] https://www.sprachenatelier-berlin.de/en/topic/3736.german-p...
children.textZhHtml:
我很欣赏你的观点，但我很好奇 B1 熟练程度实际上意味着什么，这就是我发现的 [1]：

- 理解关于工作、学校或休闲等熟悉主题的清晰标准演讲的要点 - 管理在德语地区旅行时发生的大多数情况 - 就熟悉的主题生成简单、相互关联的文本 - 描述经历、事件、梦想、希望和抱负，并简要解释您的意见或计划

对于那些想要将使用该语言的县作为永久家园的人来说，这似乎是一个合理的母语熟练程度标准。

[1] https://www.sprachenatelier-berlin.de/en/topic/3736.german-p...

children.author: TheOtherHobbes
children.textRawHtml:
B1 is a completely fair minimum standard. It's normal for many countries to expect residents to have basic conversational adequacy.

It's also the kind of requirement that's made explicit on government information about residency. So it shouldn't have been a surprise.
children.textZhHtml:
B1 是一个完全公平的最低标准。对于许多国家来说，期望居民具备基本的会话能力是很正常的。

这也是政府对居住信息提出的明确要求。所以这并不奇怪。

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml: Like, I imagine if I moved to a country and couldn’t do the B1 things in their language, I would have daily obstacles doing basic life things.
children.textZhHtml: 就像，我想象如果我搬到一个国家并且无法用他们的语言做 B1 的事情，我每天做基本生活的事情都会遇到障碍。

children.author: 14u2c
children.textRawHtml:
Not in most of Western and Central Europe. Everyone speaks great English.

It actually can be a problem because most people switch to English as soon as they realize your are not a native speaker, which can make learning harder.
children.textZhHtml:
西欧和中欧的大部分地区没有。每个人的英语都说得很好。

这实际上可能是一个问题，因为大多数人一意识到您不是母语者就改用英语，这会让学习变得更加困难。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
This was my experience. I lived for almost four years in Berlin, and was trying to learn German (I got to A2, which isn't much). But actually getting experience/immersion in German is difficult in Berlin - everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

I worked for two German companies while I was there, and the entire tech team in both companies did everything in English. This is normal there. In one of them, the (German) CEO gave a townhall speech to the entire company (~1000 employees) in English.

So yes, it's very understandable for the German government to say "you must be at least B1 to live here" but that doesn't recognise the fact that it is perfectly possible to actually live and work in Berlin with no German at all.

It's different in other German cities, apparently. If we'd moved to Munich or Cologne I would have had more pressure to learn German, and also more exposure to it and more immersion which would have helped learn it.
children.textZhHtml:
这是我的经历。我在柏林住了将近四年，一直在努力学习德语（我达到了 A2，这并不多）。但实际上在柏林获得德语的经验/沉浸式学习是很困难的 - 每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，他们就转向英语。

我在那里时曾为两家德国公司工作过，两家公司的整个技术团队都用英语做所有事情。这在那里很正常。在其中一篇文章中，（德国）首席执行官用英语向整个公司（约 1000 名员工）发表了市政厅演讲。

所以，是的，德国政府说“你必须至少达到 B1 才能住在这里”是可以理解的，但这并没有认识到一个事实，即完全没有德语也完全有可能在柏林生活和工作。

显然，这在其他德国城市是不同的。如果我们搬到慕尼黑或科隆，我会面临更大的学习德语的压力，也会有更多的接触德语和更多的沉浸感，这将有助于学习德语。

children.author: thayne
children.textRawHtml:
Even when I vacationed in Bayern (Bavaria), a lot of people switched to English when they realized that my German wasn't that good. Which was frustrating, because I wanted to practice speaking with native speakers.

Although, there were also people who only spoke German.
children.textZhHtml:
即使当我在拜仁（巴伐利亚）度假时，很多人在意识到我的德语不太好时也改用英语。这很令人沮丧，因为我想练习与母语人士交谈。

尽管如此，也有人只说德语。

children.author: doikor
children.textRawHtml: If you want to learn you just pretend that you don’t speak English and keep on speaking German. They will switch back fast.
children.textZhHtml: 如果你想学习，你就假装你不会说英语，然后继续说德语。他们会很快切换回来。

children.author: flakeoil
children.textRawHtml:
This is the right way. Speak the language you want. Even if they reply to you in English, and they know you can speak English, continue in German. They will understand. They switch to English because they want to be helpful. If you continue in German they understand you prefer it that way.

Another thing is that once you start talking with a person (who you meet on a regular basis) in one language, it is hard to switch to another language at a later date. So if you start with English, it is hard to go back to German. But if you start with German, even if it is less fluent for you, it will come natural that with that person you speak in German.
children.textZhHtml:
这是正确的方法。说您想要的语言。即使他们用英语回复您，并且他们知道您会说英语，也请继续用德语。他们会理解的。他们改用英语是因为他们想提供帮助。如果您继续使用德语，他们会理解您更喜欢这种方式。

另一件事是，一旦您开始用一种语言与某人（您经常遇到的人）交谈，以后就很难切换到另一种语言。所以如果你从英语开始，就很难再回到德语。但如果你从德语开始，即使你不太流利，你也会很自然地和那个人说德语。

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml:
Did you ask if you can move to a different city and work remotely? Even if only 1-2 years, it might really help to boost your German. Moving to Dresden, Hanover, or Hamburg would help. (German HNers can probably recommend a bunch of small cities 1-2 hours away by train/car from Berlin that will use much more German language!) You could come to the office once a week.

Another tip: Always start the conversation in German. If the person switches to English, ask them politely to speak German, and explain that you are studying German. My parents did similar when they moved to the Netherlands 50 years ago. The local shopkeepers were eager to practice English with my parents, but my parents were also eager to practice their Dutch.
children.textZhHtml:
您是否询问过是否可以搬到其他城市并远程工作？ 即使只有 1-2 年，也可能确实有助于提高您的德语。 搬到德累斯顿、汉诺威或汉堡会有帮助。 （德国人可能会推荐一些距离柏林 1-2 小时火车/汽车车程的小城市，这些城市会使用更多德语！）您可以每周来办公室一次。

另一个提示：始终用德语开始对话。 如果对方改用英语，请礼貌地请他们说德语，并解释说您正在学习德语。 50 年前，我的父母搬到荷兰时也做了类似的事情。 当地的店主很想和我的父母一起练习英语，但我的父母也很想练习荷兰语。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
That's really good advice, but I found it hard to put into practice because my German was so bad. And German isn't a language you can just hammer out a badly-formed sentence in; word position is less important than conjugation, so if you get the conjugation wrong it makes no sense at all to the listener. I did try this a few times, but everyone lost patience with the painful butchering of their language when they realised their English was a lot better than my German.

I would have moved city, but a: COVID b: my wife also had a job there c: I really loved Berlin and really enjoyed living there
children.textZhHtml:
这确实是一个很好的建议，但我发现很难付诸实践，因为我的德语太糟糕了。德语不是一种你可以敲出一个结构错误的句子的语言；词位不如词形变化重要，所以如果词形变化错误，听者就毫无意义。我确实尝试过几次，但当每个人意识到他们的英语比我的德语好得多时，他们都对语言的痛苦屠宰失去了耐心。

我本来会搬到城市，但是a：新冠病毒b：我妻子在那里也有一份工作c：我真的很喜欢柏林，真的很喜欢在那里生活

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml: I like the effort! It is more that half of the battle when learning a new language as an adult. What really helped me: Create your own custom Anki deck. Literally, start from zero. Each time you try-and-fail (or think of something that you don't know how to say), write a note to yourself in English/broken German with the sentence/idea that failed. At home, use a translate tool (LLM/Google/whatever) to build the correct German sentence. Next, add it to your Anki deck. Personally, I only add full sentences, never just vocab, because it adds more natural context. Only add real stuff that you will use. On the first day, you have an Anki deck with one card. If you are really motivated, try to add one card per day, but just 1-2 cards per week will quickly improve the deck. Since the deck is so damn small, you can blow through it in five minutes while riding the S-Bahn/U-Bahn trains. And, every single sentence is useful to you. Bonus: Ask a native German speaker about some of your Anki deck sentences. They will probably love to talk to you about the German language because you will be showing your commitment.
children.textZhHtml: 我喜欢这种努力！ 作为一个成年人，学习一门新语言就已经成功了一半。 真正帮助我的是：创建您的自己的自定义 Anki 牌组。 顾名思义，就是从零开始。 每次你尝试失败（或者想到一些你不知道怎么说的事情）时，用英语/蹩脚的德语给自己写一张纸条，写上失败的句子/想法。 在家里，使用翻译工具（法学硕士/谷歌/其他）来构建正确的德语句子。 接下来，将其添加到您的 Anki 套牌中。 就我个人而言，我只添加完整的句子，而不仅仅是词汇，因为它添加了更自然的上下文。 仅添加您将使用的真实内容。 第一天，您将拥有一套包含一张卡片的 Anki 套牌。 如果您确实有动力，请尝试每天添加一张卡，但每周只需添加 1-2 张卡即可快速改善牌组。 由于甲板实在是太小了，乘坐 S-Bahn/U-Bahn 火车只需五分钟就可以穿过它。 而且，每一句话对你都有用。 额外奖励：向以德语为母语的人询问您的 Anki 甲板句子。 他们可能会很乐意与您谈论德语，因为您会表现出您的承诺。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml:
>everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

OK maybe things have changed in recent years. I was always annoyed when coming to visit family living in Berlin for a couple days or driving in to a conference, and inevitably there would come a time when I would have to ask someone for help and I would have to ask in English because I do not speak German (although due to youthful immersion I start to remember it again after a couple of weeks) and they would inevitably answer in German.

I could somewhat understand when we stopped in a park asking where the convention center was of just a couple people by the side of the road, but asking a ticket agent at a train station what route I should take to get to a particular station and getting replies in German seemed especially rude.

At any rate despite some good experiences with Germans regarding helpfulness I have had a few too many negative experiences.
children.textZhHtml:
>每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，我就开始说英语了。

好吧，也许近年来情况已经发生了变化。当我来柏林探望几天的家人或开车去参加会议时，我总是很恼火，不可避免地会有这样的时候，我必须向某人寻求帮助，我必须用英语询问，因为我不会说德语（尽管由于年轻的沉浸感，几周后我又开始记住它），他们不可避免地会用德语回答。

当我们在公园停下来询问会议中心在哪里时，旁边只有几个人，我可以理解。路，但在火车站询问售票员我应该走哪条路线才能到达特定车站并得到德语答复似乎特别粗鲁。

无论如何，尽管德国人在帮助方面有一些良好的经验，但我还是有过太多负面的经历。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
Berliners are renowned even in Germany for being blunt to the point of rudeness.

What we in the Anglosphere would consider rude, they consider efficient. It can be very off-putting at first, but you do get used to it.
children.textZhHtml:
即使在德国，柏林人也因直率到粗鲁而闻名。

我们在英语圈中认为粗鲁的事情，在他们看来却是高效的。一开始可能会非常令人不快，但你确实会习惯它。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml: in most other countries renowned for being efficient it would not be considered efficient to answer in a language the questioner evidently does not speak.
children.textZhHtml: 在大多数其他以高效着称的国家，用提问者显然不会说的语言回答不会被认为是高效的。

children.author: atoav
children.textRawHtml:
To play the devils advocate: It is efficient — for them.

It is a bit of a myth that everybody in Germany talks English. Sure in an academic or business environment, or in certain social circles that can be taken for granted, but there are people who can only talk German or whose English is so bad they will avoid talking it.
children.textZhHtml:
唱个反调：这对他们来说确实很高效——

说德国人人都会说英语，多少有些言过其实。诚然，在学术或商业环境中，或是某些社交圈子里，这被视为理所当然，但确实有些人只会说德语，或者英语太差而尽量避免开口。

children.author: aveao
children.textRawHtml:
This isn't really true. I've lived in both Munich and Hamburg now, and you 100% need some amount of german to navigate life, many people do not in fact have conversational-level english. I often say that the common paths tend to be easy to navigate in english (e.g. booking a train ticket, getting a phone line), but the adjacent flows (e.g. filing a refund request for your unfulfilled seat reservation, cancelling your phone contract) often require talking to someone in german. A doctor may be marked as speaking english, but that doesn't mean that their reception speaks english.

Berlin may be a different story within Germany, and then there's the entirety of e.g. Netherlands, but I know that the situation in France isn't better than here.

---

That said, B1 is a difficult level within german. Being able to converse fluently enough in not-quite-correct german -which is enough for getting through day to day life- is not necessarily enough to pass the test, and you really need to crunch through memorizing the grammatical gender of thousands of words, alongside getting really good at applying them in various grammatical forms (e.g. declensions vary for the same word based on how it's used).
children.textZhHtml:
这不是真的。我现在住在慕尼黑和汉堡，你100%需要一定程度的德语来驾驭生活，很多人实际上没有会话水平的英语。我经常说，常见的路径往往很容易用英语导航（例如预订火车票、拨打电话线），但相邻的流程（例如为未完成的座位预订提出退款请求、取消电话合同）通常需要与用德语的人交谈。医生可能被标记为说英语，但这并不意味着他们的接待处说英语。

在德国柏林可能是一个不同的故事，然后还有整个例如。荷兰，但我知道法国的情况并不比这里好。

---

话虽如此，B1在德语中是一个困难的级别。能够用不完全正确的德语进行足够流利的交谈（这足以应付日常生活）并不一定足以通过测试，你确实需要记住数千个单词的语法性别，同时真正擅长以各种语法形式应用它们（例如，同一个单词的词尾变化根据其使用方式而有所不同）。

children.author: stnikolauswagne
children.textRawHtml:
There is some sort of weird phenomenon that I cant quite put to words, but the core is:

While there is a big chunk of the native german population speaking great english there is a subset of (often) service workers who... dont. Had multiple furniture movers come through my house the last few weeks, the only way to communicate with them was broken german, since they had no english skills and my (croatian? serbian?) skills were zero.

At one point I ended up in the weirdest situation of my live were i translated the german instructions from the phone rep into english to the shift lead of the moving company so he could then give them in the native language to the movers.
children.textZhHtml:
有一种奇怪的现象，我无法用语言来形容，但其核心是：

虽然有很大一部分德国本土人口能说流利的英语，但有一小部分（通常）服务人员......却不会。过去几周，有多名家具搬运工来到我家，与他们沟通的唯一方式是蹩脚的德语，因为他们没有英语技能，而我的（克罗地亚语？塞尔维亚语？）技能为零。

有一次，我陷入了一生中最奇怪的境地，我将电话代表的德语指令翻译成英语，交给搬家公司的值班领导，这样他就可以用母语将它们交给搬运工。

children.author: bulbar
children.textRawHtml:
That's basically why you should learn the language of the country you live in.

Blaming people for not speaking English in Germany is pretty weird. How about learning German instead?
children.textZhHtml:
这就是为什么你应该学习你所居住国家的语言的原因。

在德国指责人们不会说英语是很奇怪的。学习德语怎么样？

children.author: fransje26
children.textRawHtml:
> Blaming people for not speaking English in Germany is pretty weird. How about learning German instead?

As the poster wrote:

>> At one point I ended up in the weirdest situation of my live were i translated the german instructions from the phone rep into english [..]

which implies that he does, in fact, speak German. The movers did not, though.
children.textZhHtml:
> 在德国责怪别人不说英语挺奇怪的。不如自己学学德语吧？

正如发帖人所写：

>> 有次我遇到了人生中最离谱的情况，我居然把电话客服的德语指示翻译成英语[..]

这说明他其实会说德语。不过搬家工人不会。

children.author: srejk

children.textZhHtml: 我欣赏他所代表的一切 - 他几乎在所有问题上都站在正确的一边。我只是希望他能制作出更加简洁有效的视频。

children.author: darksim905
children.textRawHtml: You really want more succinct videos than what he already makes? Although they may seem long in a modern day where people have the attention span of a gnat -- his videos are fantastic and to the point. He literally ignores a lot more complexities and drama surrounding a lot of issues. Does he ramble sometimes? Maybe, but the talks so fast at times that the video is over before I've even gotten half way through with my own research. And this is from someone who has followed him since he posted videos on spinning on your own VoIP server and when he was still based in NYC.
children.textZhHtml: 您真的想要比他已经制作的视频更简洁的视频吗？尽管在当今人们的注意力集中时间有限的今天，这些视频可能看起来很长，但他的视频非常棒，而且切题。他确实忽略了围绕许多问题的更多复杂性和戏剧性。他有时会胡言乱语吗？也许吧，但有时谈话速度太快，以至于我自己的研究还没完成一半，视频就结束了。这是来自自从他在您自己的 VoIP 服务器上发布有关旋转的视频以及他还在纽约时就一直关注他的人。

children.author: Zancarius
children.textRawHtml:
"An account of human suffering" (assuming I remember the approximate title) is one of his earlier videos that sticks out in my mind and one I'll never forget. It was a tongue-in-cheek nod to the fact board diagnostics for hardware that has obviously been abused by the end user is a tedious and often thankless process.

I learned a LOT from watching his videos, and I don't even do board repair! Psychology, business, the human condition...
children.textZhHtml:
“人类苦难的叙述”（假设我记得大概的标题）是他早期的视频之一，在我的脑海中挥之不去，我永远不会忘记。这是对硬件主板诊断显然被最终用户滥用这一事实的半开玩笑的认可，这是一个乏味且常常吃力不讨好的过程。

我从观看他的视频中学到了很多东西，而且我什至不进行主板维修！心理学、商业、人类状况……

children.author: cgyvbunji
children.textRawHtml: Personally I think if he did that people wouldn't pay attention. My experience is that when you complain to people about digital rights they just glaze over unless you walk them through all the ins and outs and the implications.
children.textZhHtml: 我个人认为，如果他这样做，人们就不会注意到。我的经验是，当你向人们抱怨数字版权时，除非你向他们介绍所有的来龙去脉及其影响，否则他们只会呆呆地看着。

children.author: forinti

children.textZhHtml: 向公众通报情况是一场艰苦的战斗。大多数人只是不理解，也不愿意理解这些问题，直到问题真正发生在他们身上，例如购买京东的农民。

children.author: wickedsight

children.textZhHtml: 我是那些大多会忽略他的视频的人之一，因为它们的长度以及它们给我的“老人对事情愤怒咆哮”的氛围。

children.author: FatherOfCurses

children.author: tsumnia

children.textZhHtml: “我气得要死，再也忍不下去了！”

Ditto. I love his ideology, and have watched a few of his videos over the years, but his lengthy roundabout style of delivery doesn't keep me engaged.

Super glad he does what he does / is who he is though.
children.textZhHtml:
同感。我很认同他的理念，这些年也看过他的一些视频，但他那种冗长迂回的表达方式实在让我提不起劲。

children.author: throw2ih020
children.textRawHtml: HN audience prefers text to video; videos rarely do well here compared to other sites.
children.textZhHtml: HN的受众更喜欢文字而不是视频；与其他网站相比，视频在这里很少能取得好成绩。

children.author: spacedcowboy
children.textRawHtml:
This. I never watch video posts. I can skim-read a text site in seconds to see if it's worth a real read. I can't do that with video, and there's always some ads in it, whether the author put them there or not. I hate ads.

And it's not just HN either, video is a very poor substitute for a well written article. Video can augment a well-written article, but it can't replace it.
children.textZhHtml:
确实如此。我从来不看视频帖子。我可以在几秒钟内略读一个文字网站，看看是否值得仔细阅读。但视频做不到这一点，而且里面总是有广告，不管是不是作者自己加的。我讨厌广告。

而且这不仅仅是HN的问题，视频根本无法很好地替代一篇好文章。视频可以补充一篇好文章，但无法取代它。

children.author: pchristensen

My overall minutes of YT have gone down, but I probably interacts with 2-3x more videos. Just like skimming on HN!
children.textZhHtml:
如果你有YouTube Premium，你可以用他们的Gemini AI来总结视频。自从发现这个功能后，我“消费”的视频数量大幅增加，因为通过摘要我就能大致判断这个视频是否值得真正去看。

children.author: bookofjoe

children.author: gosub100

children.textZhHtml: 发在这里给可能不知道的人提个醒：在大多数YouTube视频上长按屏幕，它们就会以2倍速播放。我发现这是测试主持人表达是否有效的一个好方法。如果我在2倍速下能听得清清楚楚，那就说明他们说话太慢了。

comments.author: wxw
comments.textRawHtml:
> It’s designed to be extremely easy to self-host on your own infrastructure.

Kudos for this. Per the docs: https://docs.chatto.run/,

> Chatto ships in a compact, self-contained binary

> it uses NATS, a compact message broker that also ships with a built-in stream persistence engine [...] NATS is just as easy to provision as Chatto, and most of our examples will show you how.

> you can also configure an external S3-compatible object storage for Chatto to store your files in, and we strongly recommend doing so...

> The actual calls are powered by LiveKit (Apache-2.0), which you need to deploy alongside Chatto. As with NATS, the deployment examples show the required wiring.

> ...

And kudos for backing it up with real guidance. Great project.
comments.textZhHtml:
> 它的设计非常容易在您自己的基础设施上自行托管。

对此表示赞赏。根据文档：https://docs.chatto.run/，

> Chatto 以紧凑、独立的二进制形式提供

> 它使用 NATS，这是一个紧凑的消息代理，还附带内置流持久性引擎 [...] NATS 与 Chatto 一样易于配置，我们的大多数示例将向您展示如何配置。

> 您还可以配置外部与 S3 兼容的对象存储可供 Chatto 存储您的文件，我们强烈建议您这样做...

> 实际调用由 LiveKit (Apache-2.0) 提供支持，您需要将其与 Chatto 一起部署。与 NATS 一样，部署示例显示了所需的接线。

> ...

并感谢您在真正的指导下对其进行备份。伟大的项目。

children.author: lofties
children.textRawHtml: Wow, it's using NATS! I used NATS extensively 10+ years ago, and I'm happy to hear it's still around. Our infrastructure had hiccups across our fleet of machines, but one part that always remained up and running without complaining on some dinky machine was NATS. Well, that and Redis. No complaints ever.
children.textZhHtml: 哇，它正在使用 NATS！我十多年前广泛使用 NATS，很高兴听到它仍然存在。我们的基础设施在我们的机器群中出现了问题，但在某些小型机器上始终保持正常运行且没有任何抱怨的部分是 NATS。嗯，还有 Redis。没有任何抱怨。

children.author: freakynit
children.textRawHtml: We are building something B2B expected to have good enough scale ... we had to choose between kafka, nats, redpanda and rabbitMQ .... We went ahead with NATS .... don't know why, but, when we went through all the docs, including setup and operational, NATS just felt right.
children.textZhHtml: 我们正在构建一些 B2B 的东西，预计具有足够好的规模......我们必须在 kafka、nats、redpanda 和rabbitMQ 之间进行选择......我们继续选择 NATS ....不知道为什么，但是，当我们浏览所有文档（包括设置和操作）时，NATS 感觉是正确的。

children.author: bobkb
children.textRawHtml:
We are in the same decision making stage and trying to choose between rabitMQ, Kafka, NATS and some solutions built on top of Redis.

Did NATS eventually worked well?
children.textZhHtml:
我们也正处于同样的决策阶段，正在RabbitMQ、Kafka、NATS以及一些基于Redis构建的解决方案之间做选择。

NATS最终用起来效果好吗？

children.author: freakynit
children.textRawHtml:
JetStream (not NATS) has a built-in key/value store. Relevant thread (old, from 2023): https://github.com/nats-io/nats.go/discussions/1507

someone did some benchmarks in 2025:

    nats bench kv put --size="128" --msgs 1000000 --storage file
    1m3s → Pub stats: 15,656 msgs/sec ~ 1.91 MB/sec
    
    nats bench kv get --size="128" --msgs 1000000
    59s → Sub stats: 16,720 msgs/sec ~ 2.04 MB/sec
I have personally not used this though.

comments.author: pjg
comments.textRawHtml:
One of the reasons why legacy apps continue to live on (despite outliving their usefulness ) is third party tie-ins.

The reason most companies are reluctant to switch from slack is because they have external third parties i.e. their partners connected with external channels within slack. Changing their internal DM/IM system will remove their ability to communicate with their partners in the same system.

Perhaps a plugin where slack messages can be forwarded to Chatto and eventually bi-directional i.e. Chatto messages can be forwarded to Slack.
comments.textZhHtml:
遗留应用程序继续存在（尽管不再有用）的原因之一是第三方搭配。

大多数公司不愿意从 Slack 切换的原因是因为他们有外部第三方，即他们的合作伙伴与 slack 内的外部渠道连接。更改内部 DM/IM 系统将消除他们与同一系统中的合作伙伴进行通信的能力。

也许有一个插件，可以将 slack 消息转发到 Chatto，并最终实现双向，即 Chatto 消息可以转发到 Slack。

children.author: jldugger
children.textRawHtml:
I think the request here is not about sounding like Majel Barrett but in keeping the output extremely terse and unobtrusive.

There's been a few studys showing that novices love LLM output that's long, but experts hate it. As an example, I've been tasked with using some agentic PM tool to write specs, and it keeps generating these huge page long outputs with "HBR voice" bolded summaries of paragraph long bulletpoints. I.e.:

> Right-size hard, and watch the one open-ended edge. Endorse the DRI's simplifications wholesale: drop the runbook-per-alert mandate (keep 1–2 diagnostic-only runbooks for the high-priority set), and ride durability on the existing weekly incident + monthly operational reviews — no new governance. The single scope-creep risk is the coverage strand (gaps are defined by absence); bound it to gaps evidenced by real, already-missed customer-facing outages, not a proactive gap hunt. Curing ownership gaps (e.g. foo-bar, no clear owner) is finite in-scope work.

There's dozens of these every iteration. I can't imagine trying to deal with that via voice, I would just zone out after the second sentence.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为这里的要求不是听起来像 Majel Barrett，而是要保持输出极其简洁和不引人注目。

有一些研究表明，新手喜欢 LLM 输出很长，但专家讨厌它。举个例子，我的任务是使用一些代理 PM 工具来编写规范，它不断生成这些大页长的输出，并带有“HBR 语音”段落长项目符号的粗体摘要。即：

> 适当大小的硬性，并观察一个开放式边缘。大规模认可 DRI 的简化：放弃每个警报的运行手册要求（为高优先级集保留 1-2 个仅用于诊断的运行手册），并在现有的每周事件 + 每月运营审查上实现持久性 - 无需新的治理。单一范围蔓延风险是覆盖链（间隙由缺席定义）；将其与实际的、已经错过的面向客户的中断所证明的差距联系起来，而不是主动寻找差距。消除所有权差距（例如 foo-bar，没有明确的所有者）是有限的范围内工作。

每次迭代都有数十个这样的工作。我无法想象尝试通过语音来处理这个问题，我会在第二句话后走神。

children.author: bityard
children.textRawHtml:
I'm just going to say, Wow. That's a pretty incredible result. I had no idea you could clone a voice with such a short snippet of audio these days.

I've had this dream of talking to the Enterprise-D computer since I was 8 years old. Midlife-crisis me still has that dream, but hooked up to Home Assistant so it can actually do useful things too. A couple months back, I went looking around for "clean" samples of Majel's voice as the computer but didn't have a lot of luck. Even though there are three television series and several movies, pretty much all of them have some amount of background noise, bleeps, bloops, or warp core thrum. (As this one does.) There may be modern ways to clean those up without affecting her voice much, but I haven't dug into that yet.

There are a few audiobooks narrated by Majel Barrett but obviously her role as the computer was proper voice acting and so the books would not be good source material.

There were also a few games/CD-ROM (Omnipedia) with some samples, but they did not bother to post-process them for that lofi Enterprise-D computer feel. Can _probably_ be replicated fairly faithfully after the fact, but I only know a _little_ about audio post-processing.

According to her son (Rod Roddenberry), Majel sat down in a studio and recorded audio samples specifically for the purpose of having her voice cloned someday for future Star Trek stories. However, those haven't been released publicly. (And likely never will, but I can dream, can't I?)

Edit: I played with your samples and the time it takes to generate the output audio is pretty brutal. Too slow for interactive use. Maybe that's a limitation of the HF-hosted app, though.

从 8 岁起，我就一直梦想着与 Enterprise-D 计算机交谈。中年危机的我仍然有这个梦想，但连接到家庭助理，所以它实际上也可以做有用的事情。几个月前，我四处寻找 Majel 声音的“干净”样本作为计算机，但运气不佳。尽管有三部电视剧和几部电影，但几乎所有这些都有一定程度的背景噪音、嘟嘟声、杂音或曲速核心嗡嗡声。 （就像这本书一样。）可能有现代方法可以在不影响她声音的情况下清理这些内容，但我还没有深入研究。

children.author: Melonai
children.textRawHtml:
I noticed that too, I guess the content in the training data suggests that when some text self-identifies as "cold, hard logic" it additionally assumes a mean tone. Additionally it makes responses more likely to disagree even when given inputs that are more or less valid. A while ago I tested out giving some idea to a model with the "hard logic" prompt, then taking its own output, reverting the conversation and giving it its own statement again, making it disagree with the very same statement it just made itself. This was mostly just humorous, and doesn't indicate too much, after all both statements might've been wrong in some way, but the statement itself was moreso an opinion with no fully right answer, showing this tendency to disagree.

I guess when we tell models to use "cold logic" they don't interpret that purely definitionally, but moreso with what this statement usually implies, which is often a disagreement between two people, one trying to leverage supposed logic to disagree and denigrate the other party, oftentimes actually arguing out of emotion and not the logic they claim to use. This probably occurs enough to give model responses a mean tint. That's my theory on why this could occur at least.
children.textZhHtml:
我也注意到了这一点，我猜训练数据中的内容表明，当某些文本自我识别为“冷酷、强硬的逻辑”时，它还会呈现出刻薄的语气。此外，即使给出的输入或多或少有效，它也会使响应更有可能出现不一致。不久前，我测试了用“硬逻辑”提示向模型提供一些想法，然后获取其自己的输出，恢复对话并再次给出自己的陈述，使其与刚刚自己做出的陈述不一致。这主要只是幽默，并没有说明太多，毕竟这两种陈述可能在某种程度上都是错误的，但陈述本身更多的是一种没有完全正确答案的观点，显示出这种不同意的倾向。

我想当我们告诉模型使用“冷逻辑”时，他们不会纯粹从定义上解释这一点，更重要的是这个陈述通常意味着什么，这通常是两个人之间的分歧，一个人试图利用所谓的逻辑来反对和诽谤对方，通常实际上是这样争论是出于情感，而不是他们声称使用的逻辑。这种情况的发生可能足以使模型响应呈现出平均的色调。这就是我关于为什么这种情况至少会发生的理论。

children.author: cortesoft
children.textRawHtml: Yep, and this is a perfect example of a base rate fallacy situation... even if the scanner is 99.99% accurate, because an even higher percentage of photos are innocent, most matches the scanner will find will be false positives.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，这是基本率谬误情况的完美示例...即使扫描仪的准确率达到 99.99%，因为更高比例的照片是无辜的，扫描仪发现的大多数匹配都将是误报。

children.author: wesammikhail
children.textRawHtml:
Funny you bring this up.

Back in the day when I was like 15 and DC++ was still a thing, I used to browse people's shared folders. One day I came across a file called "the paradox of false positive". It was a 1 pager that described how a machine which is 99.9% accurate at identifying terrorists would be completely useless due to this false positive base rate fallacy you're describing.

It really stuck with me throughout the years. It's kind o remarkable how even a 99.9% accurate heuristic is insufficient at scale.

Which begs the question: lets assume the intentions are pure (which we know they're not but lets be generous), what other options are there when 99.9% heuristic is not good enough? how do you design systems when they're guaranteed to fail as they scale up?

edit: and what do you know, I just saw this as I scrolled down on HN https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48816959
children.textZhHtml:
有趣的是你提到了这个。

当我 15 岁的时候，DC++ 还很流行，我曾经浏览人们的共享文件夹。有一天，我看到一份名为“误报悖论”的文件。这是一个 1 寻呼机，它描述了一台识别恐怖分子准确率达到 99.9% 的机器如何由于您所描述的误报基本率谬误而变得完全无用。

多年来，它一直困扰着我。值得注意的是，即使是 99.9% 准确的启发式方法在规模上也是不够的。

这就引出了一个问题：让我们假设意图是纯粹的（我们知道它们不是，但让我们慷慨一些），当 99.9% 启发式方法不够好时，还有哪些其他选择？当系统规模扩大时注定会失败时，你如何设计系统？

编辑：你知道吗，我在 HN https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48816959

children.author: DiggyJohnson

I genuinely think it’s all three:

1. The cultural factor is rising expectations for children and their parents, costing both time and money;

2. The political/social factor is nanny states and academic institutions that the public expects to not only teach but raise their kids;

3. Technology. Especially the Internet, mobile devices, social media, and short form content. Technology distracts and isolates both kids and parents.

An example of the three factors at work is the all-too-common local news trope of ”Nosy neighbor calls CPS because the family next door lets their kids walk to school. Whole family traumatized as a result.”

1.文化因素是对孩子及其父母的期望不断提高，这会花费时间和金钱；

2。政治/社会因素是保姆国家和学术机构，公众不仅期望它们教育孩子，还期望它们抚养孩子；

3。技术。尤其是互联网、移动设备、社交媒体和简短内容。技术会分散孩子和家长的注意力并使其孤立。

这三个因素的一个例子是当地非常常见的新闻比喻：“爱管闲事的邻居打电话给 CPS，因为隔壁的家庭让他们的孩子步行上学。结果整个家庭都受到了创伤。”

stories.title: GLM 5.2 and the coming AI margin collapse
stories.titleZh: GLM 5.2 和即将到来的人工智能利润率崩溃

comments.author: fny
comments.textRawHtml:
I'm not convinced raw costs matter:

1. Compute costs collapsed since the advent of Cloud and yet hyperscalers still have fat margins.

2. Many open source office suites exist yet none compete with the ubiquity of gsuite or office. GitHub, Slack are similar examples.

3. Both Windows and macOS dominate the home desktop space despite free alternatives existing for a long time.

4. Many formerly open source infrastructure components like Redis and Elastic Search have Apache equivalents, but they still command healthy margins.

I understand the arguments for a margin collapse, but I don't see any historical analogues. It seems that enterprises will pay top dollar for service guarantees, integration, and someone they can sue.

It's nobody gets fired for buying IBM all over again.
comments.textZhHtml:
我不相信原始成本很重要：

1.自云出现以来，计算成本大幅下降，但超大规模企业仍然拥有丰厚的利润。

2.存在许多开源办公套件，但没有一个可以与无处不在的 gsuite 或 Office 竞争。 GitHub、Slack 都是类似的例子。

3.尽管免费替代品早已存在，但 Windows 和 macOS 都在家庭桌面领域占据主导地位。

4.许多以前的开源基础设施组件（如 Redis 和 Elastic Search）都有 Apache 的同类组件，但它们仍然拥有健康的利润。

我理解利润崩溃的论点，但我没有看到任何历史类似物。企业似乎愿意为服务保证、集成以及可以起诉的人支付高额费用。

没有人会因为再次购买 IBM 而被解雇。

children.author: nostrademons
children.textRawHtml:
There's a huge case of survivorship bias when trying to recall historical analogues, because in every instance where margins collapsed and competition made the industry a commodity business, the big proprietary names are no longer with us. Here's a selection of examples, though:

1. Memory chip margins collapsed so much in the 80s that Intel exited the memory chip business entirely. At the time, they were known much more as a memory chip company than a microprocessor company.

2. Margins for high-end workstations collapsed in the face of cheaper IBM PC clones and an explosion of MS Windows software. This led directly to the deaths of SGI, Sun, Symbolics, Lucid, LMI, etc.

3. Proprietary UNIX variants like HP-UX, IRIX, AIX, and SCO Unix have basically completely died out, replaced by lower-cost proprietary OSes like Windows and MacOS, or by open-source descendants of Linux and BSD.

4. Many commercial database vendors like Oracle, dBase, Sybase, FoxPro, and Microsoft (SQL Server and Access) found themselves very much under margin pressure from PostGres, MySQL, and SQLite. Oracle survived thanks to their massive installed base and legal department, and Microsoft survived because they could cross-subsidize from their OS and Office monopolies, but dBase, Sybase, and FoxPro are no longer with us.
children.textZhHtml:
在试图回忆历史类似事件时，存在着巨大的幸存者偏差，因为在每一次利润锐减和竞争使该行业成为商品业务的情况下，大的专有名称都不再属于我们。 不过，这里是一些示例的选择：

1.内存芯片利润在 80 年代大幅下滑，以至于英特尔完全退出了内存芯片业务。 当时，他们更多地被认为是一家内存芯片公司，而不是一家微处理器公司。

2。面对更便宜的 IBM PC 克隆产品和 MS Windows 软件的爆炸式增长，高端工作站的利润大幅下降。 这直接导致了SGI、Sun、Symbolics、Lucid、LMI等的消亡。

3。 HP-UX、IRIX、AIX 和 SCO Unix 等专有 UNIX 变体基本上已经完全消失，取而代之的是 Windows 和 MacOS 等成本较低的专有操作系统，或者 Linux 和 BSD 的开源后代。

4。许多商业数据库供应商，如 Oracle、dBase、Sybase、FoxPro 和 Microsoft (SQL Server and Access) 发现自己面临来自 PostGres、MySQL 和 SQLite 的利润压力。 Oracle 幸存下来要归功于其庞大的安装基础和法律部门，而 Microsoft 幸存下来是因为他们可以从其操作系统和 Office 垄断中进行交叉补贴，但 dBase、Sybase 和 FoxPro 已不再属于我们。

children.author: corford
children.textRawHtml:
It's politics.

The US's corporate problems in the 1980s and early 1990s existed when strong international competition existed.

It began to change with things like https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1986_U.S.%E2%80%93Japan_Semico... and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plaza_Accord.

It was accelerated further with things like anti-circumvention clauses in Free Trade agreements (see Cory Doctrow's recent highlighting of this: https://pluralistic.net/2026/01/01/39c3/) and then had more gasoline thrown on the fire in the ZIRP/easy money era post GFC, culminating with the bazooka of stimulus unleashed post-covid.

My best guess is we are now going to witness ~20 years of slow unwind. You can already see signs of this in things like RoW/EM stocks outperforming the S&P, treasury yields diverging from other "safe haven" soverign bonds (e.g. swiss), gold price rising, Europe starting to get serious about addressing the Draghi report's findings, European defence spending increasing, China starting to act like the "adult in the room" wrt the recent Iran/US blow-up etc. Essentially, countries/blocs attempting to re-assert sovereignty that has been willingly diluted over the last ~30 years to mainly America's benefit.
children.textZhHtml:
这是政治。

美国在 20 世纪 80 年代和 1990 年代初存在企业问题，当时国际竞争激烈。

随着 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1986_U.S.%E2%80%93Japan_Semico... 和 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plaza_Accord。

自由贸易协定中的反规避条款等进一步加速了这种趋势（参见 Cory Doctrow 最近强调的这一点：https://pluralistic.net/2026/01/01/39c3/），然后在全球金融危机后的 ZIRP/宽松货币时代火上浇油，最终以刺激措施的火箭筒而达到顶峰后新冠疫情爆发。

我最好的猜测是，我们现在将见证约 20 年的缓慢放松。你已经可以在以下方面看到这种迹象：世界其他地区/新兴市场股票的表现优于标准普尔指数，国债收益率与其他“避险”主权债券（例如瑞士债券）不同，金价上涨，欧洲开始认真对待德拉吉报告的调查结果，欧洲国防支出增加，中国开始在最近的伊朗/美国冲突中表现得像“房间里的成年人”等等。重新主张在过去约 30 年里为了主要美国的利益而自愿淡化的主权。

children.author: nostrademons
children.textRawHtml:
Somewhere else in the comments here, someone else remarked "Individuals perhaps [move to the new models], but not organizations."

That's illustrative. The mechanism by which organizations are forced to update their technology, move to more competitive suppliers, and cut costs is a recession. In one, every business that doesn't do so goes bankrupt, and what's left are the more efficient businesses that have adopted technology effectively.

We haven't had a real recession since 2009. (2020 was an odd case, because it was effectively brought on by government edict and so it actually killed a number of efficient but unlucky sectors while doing nothing to clean out the dead wood in major corporations). The next one is likely to be a doozy, because the economy is filled with bullshit jobs, bullshit corporations, and bullshit products.
children.textZhHtml:
在此处评论的其他地方，其他人评论道“个人可能[转向新模型]，但不是组织。”

这很说明问题。 组织被迫更新技术、转向更有竞争力的供应商并削减成本的机制是衰退。 其一，每家不这样做的企业都会破产，剩下的是有效采用技术的效率更高的企业。

自 2009 年以来，我们还没有经历过真正的衰退。（2020 年是一个奇怪的例子，因为它是它实际上是由政府法令引发的，因此它实际上杀死了一些高效但不幸的部门，同时没有采取任何措施来清理大公司的死木）。 下一个可能会很疯狂，因为经济充满了狗屎工作、狗屎公司和狗屎产品。

children.textRawHtml:
The case of Sweden entirely falsifies the theory that "it would've been much worse without government action."

No government action whatsoever was taken in Sweden. None. Death rates were no better or worse than adjacent countries that intervened extensively.

children.textZhHtml:

瑞典没有采取任何政府行动。完全没有。其死亡率与那些采取大规模干预措施的邻国相比，既没有更好，也没有更糟。

因此，无论如何，政府干预都是无关紧要的。

With decisive government action (see New Zealand), millions less people would have died, and the economy would have done better.

children.author: Xunjin

children.author: etcimon
children.textRawHtml:
There's multiple ways to look at the economy, the raw exchange of dollar currency in a debt chase (shit I need to run faster & pay down this stuff!), there's the productivity of industrial output (shit we need to sell more junk and useless crap!), and there's the stock and productivity in optimal life cycles (Damn, that Nokia is a tank!)

If 10% of the population went away, it would affect 1 & 2, but in any true practical lens, there's a ton of cheap empty houses, while on the other hand building repairable stuff that lasts or enough cheaply is where economies move to more complex technologies by saving time and effort in useless endeavour of debt chases or consumption-oriented wasteful productivity
children.textZhHtml:
有多种方式来看待经济，债务追逐中美元货币的原始兑换（该死的我需要跑得更快并偿还这些东西！），有工业产出的生产力（该死的我们需要出售更多垃圾和无用的垃圾！），以及最佳生命周期中的库存和生产力（该死，诺基亚是一辆坦克！）

如果 10% 的人口离开，它将影响 1 & 2，但在任何真正实用的镜头中，都有大量廉价的空置房屋，而另一方面，建造持久或足够便宜的可修复的东西是经济转向更复杂的技术的地方，通过在无用的债务追逐或消费导向的浪费生产力中节省时间和精力

children.author: pocksuppet
children.textRawHtml: Economies are built by rich people for rich people. A few million poor people dead doesn't really make a dent in them.
children.textZhHtml: 经济是由富人建立并为富人服务的。几百万穷人死去，根本伤不到他们的元气。

comments.author: pier25
comments.textRawHtml:
Personally I don't care that they used AI to rewrite Bun to Rust. Even if 1.4 is not good enough it will probably get better over time.

What has pushed me back to Node is seeing how amateurish the transition has been handled.

- No LTS support for the Zig version regarding CVEs etc.

- Huge bugs like the 3MB memory leak mentioned in the blog post abandoned in the Zig version to basically force people into the Rust version to fix their apps in production.

- Zero involvement with the Bun community about such a major decision. One day it was "stop the drama I'm just playing with this" and a couple of days later "yolo merged to main".

Jarred basically keeps operating as if he was a lone hacker working on his personal project.
comments.textZhHtml:
我个人并不关心他们使用 AI 将 Bun 重写为 Rust。即使 1.4 不够好，随着时间的推移，它可能会变得更好。

让我回到 Node 的是看到过渡的处理是多么业余。

- 在 CVE 等方面，Zig 版本没有 LTS 支持。

- Zig 版本中废弃的博客文章中提到的 3MB 内存泄漏等巨大错误，基本上迫使人们使用 Rust 版本来修复生产中的应用程序。

- 与 Bun 的零参与社会对如此重大的决定。有一天，“别再闹了，我只是在玩这个”，几天后“yolo 合并到了 main”。

Jarred 基本上一直在运作，就好像他是一个孤独的黑客，致力于他的个人项目。

children.author: zamadatix
children.textRawHtml:
Getting to an LTS release train of bun is probably a good idea, assuming they are still interested in external adoption rates. They shipped 1.0 a little under 3 years ago and the acquisition has had ~6 months to get settled. Looking at an LTS release would enable a lot of the more slower moving places to look at bun within being worried about getting caught up in the velocity.

I don't know it makes sense to try to make the an LTS version of where the Zig version left off, particularly if they know they are shipping a different solution & codebase to tackle the bulk of their security and memory bugs. Let this settle a few more months with the intent of releasing an LTS of it by the end of the year, if there really is demand for it. All of the benefits of having an LTS version like Java, Node, and .NET without needing to jump to a pre-port version of the codebase which was never targeted to be a good version to LTS in the first place.

A final Zig-based LTS would have made great sense if LTS releases had already been in the picture though.
children.textZhHtml:
假设他们仍然对外部采用率感兴趣，那么进入 Bun 的 LTS 版本可能是个好主意。他们在不到 3 年前发布了 1.0，并且收购花了大约 6 个月的时间来解决。考虑 LTS 版本将使许多发展较慢的地方能够担心陷入速度之中。

我不知道尝试在 Zig 版本停止的地方制作 LTS 版本是否有意义，特别是如果他们知道他们正在发布不同的解决方案和代码库来解决大部分安全和内存错误。让这个问题再稳定几个月，如果确实有需求的话，我们打算在年底之前发布它的 LTS。拥有 Java、Node 和 .NET 等 LTS 版本的所有好处，无需跳转到代码库的预移植版本，而代码库从一开始就没有被定位为 LTS 的良好版本。

如果 LTS 版本已经在计划中，那么最终的基于 Zig 的 LTS 将非常有意义。

children.author: woodruffw
children.textRawHtml:
I don’t follow how it would be in Anthropic’s interests to maintain an LTS version of Bun. It seems unlikely they’d use it, and it would cost them money (in the form of labor).

To my understanding, Java and .NET offer public LTSes because they’re financially buoyed by huge companies using those LTS versions, and Oracle and Microsoft are fundamentally selling that LTS support as a product. I have no idea why Node does, maybe it’s similar. But Bun’s position appears to be different than all three.
children.textZhHtml:
我不明白维护Bun的LTS版本怎么符合Anthropic的利益。他们似乎不太可能使用它，而且这会耗费他们的资金（以人力的形式）。

据我了解，Java和.NET提供公开的LTS版本，是因为使用这些LTS版本的大公司为他们提供了资金支持，而且Oracle和微软本质上是把LTS支持作为一种产品来销售的。我不知道Node为什么这么做，也许情况类似。但Bun的处境似乎与这三者都不同。

children.author: atonse
children.textRawHtml:
LTS is more relevant if there was any kind of compatibility that was broken. They still haven’t released 1.4 even though it seems to have gone extremely well by every metric in the wild, with tons of people using Claude code with no regressions in a month. Nothing to me suggests they’re being careless here.

In fact, he had two adversarial reviewer Claude instances on every code change, every line. I don’t know a single human team that does two independent reviews of every line, except maybe the people that wrote space shuttle software.

Also they fixed the memory leak. How does it matter what language it’s written in? At the end of the day, people use it to run their typescript code among other things.

How many bun users care that’s it’s written in zig? I certainly don’t. I’ve been using bun for 2 years and I think I looked up zig once. It’s just not relevant.

Did it get more stable? Yes. Slimmer? Yes. More performant? Yes. Is there any proof that it got LESS secure? No. The code has been out for two months. By now all the nay sayers would’ve found the smoking gun. They haven’t. How much more proof would you like that this was a resounding success?

This is our new reality. The agents are so good that projects like this are in the realm of possible. That’s exciting.
children.textZhHtml:
如果存在任何类型的兼容性被破坏的情况，LTS 会更相关。他们仍然没有发布 1.4，尽管从各个指标来看，它似乎都运行得非常好，有大量的人使用 Claude 代码，一个月内没有出现任何回归。 在我看来，没有任何迹象表明他们在这里粗心。

事实上，他在每次代码更改、每一行上都有两个敌对的审阅者克劳德实例。我不知道有哪个人类团队会对每一行进行两次独立审查，除了编写航天飞机软件的人之外。

他们还修复了内存泄漏。它用什么语言编写有什么关系？归根结底，人们用它来运行打字稿代码等。

有多少 Bun 用户关心它是用 Zig 编写的？我当然不知道。我已经使用bun有两年了，我想我查过zig一次。它只是不相关。

它变得更稳定了吗？是的。更苗条？是的。性能更强？是的。有任何证据表明它的安全性较低吗？不。该代码已经发布两个月了。到现在为止，所有反对者都已经找到了确凿的证据。他们没有。您还需要多少证据来证明这是一次巨大的成功？

这是我们的新现实。代理商非常优秀，这样的项目是有可能实现的。这太令人兴奋了。

children.author: pier25
children.textRawHtml:

> Nothing to me suggests they’re being careless here.

Plenty of reasons suggest this including the lack of an LTS or any kind of thought put into such a massive transition.
children.textZhHtml:
> 如果存在任何类型的兼容性被破坏，LTS 更相关

我们是否 100% 确定情况并非如此？不。

> 对我来说，没有任何迹象表明他们在这里粗心。

children.author: atonse
children.textRawHtml:
Again, the code’s been out for two months. And by many accounts, many people would clearly love to scream loudly about all the things that broke.

There were initial analyses done on the port. And things continue to get refactored. But is there any slam dunk article where someone actually found any regression in functionality or stability? We’re seeing the opposite. Dozens of bugs fixed. We don’t have to theorize. They’ve been running this experiment for 2 months, with all the code out in the open.

It just feels like after two months, people want to cling to the _idea_ that this was reckless, without evidence of any meaningful negative impact.
children.textZhHtml:
再说一遍，代码已经公开两个月了。而且据很多人说，很多人显然很乐意大声声讨所有出问题的地方。

对这次移植已经做了初步分析。而且代码还在继续重构。但是有没有哪篇铁证如山的文章，是有人真的发现了功能或稳定性上的回退？我们看到的情况恰恰相反。修复了几十个bug。我们不需要凭空推测。他们已经进行了两个月的实验，所有代码都公开透明。

只是感觉两个月过去了，人们还是想死死抱住这个_想法_，认为这太草率了，却拿不出任何造成重大负面影响的证据。

children.author: srdjanr
children.textRawHtml:
I have a few opinions on this:

1) My (and possibly other people's) last impression of this was when it was merged just based on all tests passing. In many projects, relying just on tests would definitely be reckless (not sure about coverage/quality of Bun tests).

I didn't follow much what where they doing later, maybe it is indeed good enough. For example, Claude Code using it and being fine is reassuring.

2) Making such a big decision that quickly and not (even having the time to) consulting community doesn't really inspire confidence.

3) You can be reckless even if everything ends up being perfect in the end.

Going 80mph in a city, you probably have like 80% chance of not crashing. And if you don't crash, you just had a much faster and more fun trip. Doesn't mean it wasn't reckless.
children.textZhHtml:
我对此有一些看法：

1）我（可能还有其他人）对此的最后印象是当它仅基于所有通过的测试进行合并时。在很多项目中，仅仅依靠测试肯定是鲁莽的（不确定Bun测试的覆盖率/质量）。

我没有太关注他们后来做了什么，也许确实足够好。例如，克劳德·科德（Claude Code）使用它并且表现良好令人放心。

2）快速做出如此重大的决定（甚至没有时间）咨询社区并不能真正激发信心。

3）即使最终一切都很完美，你也可能会鲁莽。

在城市中以 80 英里/小时的速度行驶，你可能有 80% 的机会不撞车。如果你没有撞车，你就会有一个更快、更有趣的旅程。并不意味着它不是鲁莽的。

comments.author: padjo
comments.textRawHtml: It's very odd how quickly people fall back on emotional claims to attack this. Like we're engineers, if you can point at concrete problems with this rewrite I'd love to hear them. Obviously Jared is going to give the positive case, saying that he's doing that doesn't prove the rewrite is a bad idea. You need to point at objective problems, not your vague sense of unease. As it stands, by all available measures, this appears to have been a massive success, which is absolutely remarkable.
comments.textZhHtml: 人们很快就依靠情感主张来攻击这一点，这很奇怪。就像我们是工程师一样，如果您能指出此重写的具体问题，我很乐意听到。显然，贾里德会给出积极的例子，他说他这样做并不能证明重写是一个坏主意。你需要指出客观问题，而不是你模糊的不安感。就目前而言，从所有可用的衡量标准来看，这似乎是一个巨大的成功，这绝对是非凡的。
